turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-365, Medical Marijuana v. Horn.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, apologies to those who heard the name of this case and thought it was about marijuana, not quite. This one is about the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act which I’ll call RICO. One provision of the RICO statute allows people who have been injured by serious criminal activity to sue in Federal Court and recover triple damages. But before I get too deep into the law, let me back up and give you the facts. The plaintiff in this case is Douglas Horn, who is a commercial truck driver. Several years ago, Horn was involved in a trucking accident and he injured his back and shoulder. Months later he was still suffering from chronic pain and he began searching for natural alternatives. In his search, he discovered Dixie X, a CBD tincture produced by Medical Marijuana, Inc. Now remember that Horn worked as a long haul truck driver, a positive drug test could cost him his job so he was careful to make sure that Dixie X was THC-free. Let me give an aside for those of you who don’t know these details. Both CBD and THC are chemical compounds that naturally occur in the cannabis plant, but only THC makes you high. So, it is the THC not CBD that the Federal Government primarily seeks to regulate and it is THC not CBD that will generally trigger a positive drug test. With that background in mind, you can understand why Horn was nervous that Dixie X might contain THC, but based on his research, Dixie X seemed safe. It was described as a CBD rich non-psycho active medicine that is 0% THC. Medical Marijuan’s website promised that Dixie X was legal to consume both in the United States and in many other countries too, and a customer service representative confirmed both points. So, Horn bought a bottle and he gave it a try. His confidence was misplaced. A few weeks later, a random drug test detected THC in Horn’s system and he lost his job. That’s where RICO, the statute I mentioned at the beginning comes in. That statute allows and I’m quoting, "any person injured in his business or properly", by reason of a substantive RICO violation to sue and to recover triple damages. Horn sued Medical Marijuana under this provision, what I’ll call Civil RICO. According to his complaint, Medical Marijuana violated RICO by fraudulently advertising that Dixie X was THC-free and Horn alleged that Medical Marijuana’s violation had injured his business because he lost his job. The District Court granted summary judgment in Medical Marijuana’s favor. Civil RICO permits recovery only for business or property injuries, the court held, but in the court’s view, Horn had suffered a personal injury, the inadvertent consumption of THC, and Horn could not recovery for his lost wages the court reasoned, because they flowed from that personal injury. This conclusion reflected the view of the Sixth, Seventh and Eleventh Circuits, which hold that Civil RICO does not allow a plaintiff to recover for economic harms that flow from or are derivative of personal injuries. The Second Circuit reversed. First, it concluded that Horn had suffered a business injury. He lost his job. And disagreeing with its sister circuits, the Second Circuit held that it didn’t matter whether Horn’s business injury resulted from his personal injury, the consumption of the THC. As the Second Circuit put it, Congress expressly authorized a person to sue for business or property injuries and business or property are no less injured simply because the person has also suffered a personal injury in the causal chain. We agree with the Second Circuit. A plaintiff can seek damages for business or property loss regardless of whether that loss resulted from a personal injury. This answer is compelled by the ordinary meaning of the world injure. Lay and legal dictionaries alike define injure to mean to cause harm or damage to or to hurt. So, by creating a cause of action for a person injured in his business or property, Civil RICO allows that person to sue for all business or property harms and it excludes recovery only for personal harms. In other words, Civil RICO’s injury requirement operates with respect to the kinds of harms for which a person can recover not the cause of the harm for which he seeks relief. Medical Marijuana takes a different view. Sure it says injury means harm, but sometimes the word injury refers to the invasion of a legal right. For the lawyers in the room, that’s a tort. So, in Medical Marijuana’s view, Civil RICO permits recovery for business or property torts while excluding recovery for personal torts, and if a personal tort results in a business or property harm, it says, the plaintiff can’t recover for that harm. But as I have already mentioned, several legal dictionaries define injury to mean harm or damage and our precedent has long recognized that Civil RICO permits recovery for business or property harms. We have said that repeatedly so accepting Medical Marijuana's tort-based definition of injury would require us to make a U-turn. In an attempt to bolster its argument, Medical Marijuana turns to another word in the statute, damages. Civil RICO permits an injured person to recover three-fold the damages he sustains. As Medical Marijuana correctly notes, when Congress uses two potentially synonymous words in the same statute, we usually interpret those words to have different meanings. That rule does not help Medical Marijuana here. In its view, injured refers to the violation of a legal right where as damages refers to the loss or harm that results from this violation. While we agree with Medical Marijuana about the need to give injured and damages different meanings, we disagree with its proposed solution. Injured as we have said means harm while damages refers to monetary redress, in other words, the dollar amount that a successful plaintiff wins at the end of her suit So, we can give injured its ordinary meaning while still respective Congress’ choice to use both injured and damages in the same statute. Besides, Medical Marijuana’s own approach to the statute makes little sense. Speaking out of both sides of its mouth, it tells us that plaintiffs cannot recover for economic harms that derive from personal injury, but out of the other, it assures us that plaintiffs recover in the scenario like this. A carwash owner can sue to recovery for his business injury if a mobsters assault, a personal injury causes him to do business with a mob. That’s a little difficult to reconcile with Medical Marijuana’s own rule and that also reveals a glaring whole in Medical Marijuana’s argument. It has almost nothing to say about how courts should determine whether the plaintiff has suffered a qualifying invasion of a legal right. Because the Second Circuit reached the same conclusion, we affirm its judgment. Justice Jackson has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Kavanaugh has also filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice and Justice Alito joined.
